name: Unnamed Location (Melanite)
short_description: Seek melanite garnet in the mineral-rich rocks of Hot Spring County. A flat terrain site offering collectors the chance to find black tourmaline and quartz specimens alongside rare garnet varieties.
description:
This unnamed rockhounding location in Hot Spring County, Arkansas offers collectors access to melanite garnet and associated minerals in a geologically interesting area. The site sits on relatively flat terrain that makes it accessible to rockhounders of varying skill levels. Melanite, a variety of andradite garnet prized for its deep black color and lustrous finish, occurs here as part of the broader garnet-rich mineralogy of the Ouachita Mountains province.

The Hot Spring County area lies within the Ouachita orogeny, a region of metamorphic and igneous rocks uplifted during the Paleozoic. Melanite garnet typically forms in contact metamorphic zones and skarn deposits where magmatic activity altered carbonate and silicate rocks. The presence of melanite here reflects deep crustal processes that created ideal conditions for garnet crystallization. Associated minerals like tourmaline and quartz often accompany melanite in these deposits.

The flat topography makes this site more forgiving than many rocky Ozark locations. Collectors can expect to find loose specimens and material in weathered outcrops. The area's geology supports year-round collecting, though spring and fall offer the most comfortable conditions. Bring tools for breaking apart rock and be prepared to sample several nearby areas to locate the best specimen material.

This location remains relatively undocumented in major mineral databases, making it a potentially productive spot for serious collectors seeking garnet specimens. The combination of accessible terrain and melanite mineralization makes it worth investigating for those exploring Arkansas rockhounding opportunities.
history: Hot Spring County sits within the Ouachita Mountains, a fold-and-thrust belt created during the Paleozoic Ouachita orogeny. Melanite garnet formed in contact metamorphic zones where Precambrian and Paleozoic rocks were altered by magmatic intrusions, creating the skarn and metamorphic conditions necessary for high-quality garnet crystallization.
tips: Search weathered rock outcrops and talus slopes for loose melanite specimens - the black lustrous crystals stand out against lighter host rock. Bring a 3-5 pound hammer and cold chisel to break apart promising samples; melanite is hard but brittle, so work carefully to avoid shattering specimens. Look for melanite associated with tourmaline (black needles) and quartz veins, which indicate the contact zones most likely to yield good material. Test specimens in bright sunlight - true melanite shows deep black coloration and will not be translucent.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain typical of Hot Spring County foothills. Elevation approximately 300-400 feet. Vegetation includes mixed hardwood forest typical of the Ouachita region. Walking distance from parking should be moderate, with footing that may be uneven due to scattered rocks and forest debris. Sturdy hiking boots recommended but site is not exceptionally rugged.
best_season: March - May, September - November. These periods offer comfortable temperatures and moderate humidity. Summer heat and humidity can be intense in Arkansas lowlands; winter can be muddy. Spring and fall provide the most pleasant collecting conditions with lower risk of severe weather.
written_directions: From Rockport, head east on AR-227 toward Hot Spring County's mineral-rich terrain. The GPS coordinates (34.452146, -92.864972) point to an unmarked location approximately 8-10 miles northeast of Rockport. Use GPS navigation to reach the coordinates directly. Access may be via county roads or private property boundaries in this rural area - verify access before visiting and obtain landowner permission if collecting on private land.
road_conditions: Likely unpaved county road or private drive. Passenger vehicles may be suitable, but road conditions in rural Hot Spring County can vary seasonally. Check conditions before visiting.
parking_notes: No formal parking area documented. Expect informal roadside or field parking near the site. Capacity and amenities unknown.
collection_rules: This location appears to be on private land. Obtain explicit permission from the property owner before collecting. Respect all private property boundaries and gates. Remove only what you collect and leave no damage to structures or vegetation.
rules:
Private land - obtain landowner permission before entering or collecting
No commercial collection
Remove only hand specimens
Respect property boundaries and gates
nearest_services: Rockport is approximately 8-10 miles west. Hot Springs, Arkansas (city) offers full services including gas, restaurants, and supplies approximately 15 miles away.
accessibility_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers in moderate physical condition. Footing is uneven with scattered rocks and forest debris; sturdy footwear required. No wheelchair access. Site is not exceptionally strenuous but requires comfort with unimproved outdoor terrain.

faq.question: Where can I find melanite garnet near Rockport, Arkansas?
faq.answer: This unnamed location in Hot Spring County offers melanite garnet in contact metamorphic zones and skarn deposits. The flat terrain and weathered outcrops make it accessible for collectors. The site is approximately 8-10 miles northeast of Rockport near the GPS coordinates provided. Verify access with the landowner before visiting, as it appears to be private property.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at this Arkansas site free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: This is a private land location with no documented fee system. You must obtain permission from the property owner before collecting any specimens. There are no entrance fees associated with this site, but access depends entirely on landowner consent.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect garnet and tourmaline in Hot Spring County?
faq.answer: Bring a 3-5 pound hammer, cold chisel, hand lens, and collection bag. Melanite garnet is hard (7.5 Mohs) but brittle, so work carefully when extracting specimens. A paintbrush helps remove soil from loose samples. Sturdy gloves protect your hands from sharp rock edges and crystal terminations.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for melanite near Rockport?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer the most comfortable collecting conditions. These periods avoid Arkansas summer heat and humidity while minimizing winter mud. Avoid summer when temperatures and humidity are intense, and be cautious in winter when muddy conditions make terrain slippery.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and kids to this Arkansas rockhounding location?
faq.answer: Yes, the flat terrain and accessible collecting make this moderate-difficulty site suitable for families with children ages 8 and up. However, obtain landowner permission first. Supervise children closely around loose rocks and sharp mineral specimens. The site has no developed infrastructure, so bring water and plan for a self-sufficient outing.

faq.question: How do I identify melanite garnet when rockhounding in Arkansas?
faq.answer: Melanite is a black variety of andradite garnet characterized by deep black coloration, high luster, and a cubic crystal system. It is opaque and non-translucent, distinguishing it from other black minerals. Look for melanite associated with tourmaline (black needles) and quartz in contact zones. Hardness of 7.5 and brittleness help confirm identity - scratch tests on feldspar can verify garnet hardness.
